text: Robotic splenectomy in a patient with massive splenomegaly due to hereditary spherocytosis. We present a twenty-three year old male with history of hereditary spherocytosis and chronic anemia with the need of iron fusions. He also has postprandial bloating and intermittent upper abdominal discomfort. On the physical exam of the abdomen, there is a palpable spleen below the left costal margin. The patient underwent vaccines preoperatively. Pre-op CAT scan shows a large spleen covering the entire left upper quadrant with sizes of over sixteen centimeters anterior posterior and longitudinal.

The patient is positioned in the lateral decubitus position for a robotic splenectomy. The surgical setup is visible, including trocar placement. The spleen is retracted, and the gastroesplenic ligament is divided. The surgeon dissects and divides the splenocolic and splenorenal ligaments, followed by the dissection of the phrenosplenic ligament. The spleen is then elevated medially.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the tail of the pancreas using sharp instruments, while the short gastric vessels are ligated with bipolar energy. Careful sharp and blunt dissection is performed to detach the tail of the pancreas from the splenic hilum, avoiding injury to the pancreatic capsule. The splenic hilum is meticulously dissected, with branches of the splenic vein identified, dissected, and individually ligated.

The tail of the pancreas is densely attached and is being carefully dissected free from the surrounding tissue. The pancreas is fully mobilized, and a GIA vascular stapler is used to transect the hyaline. The specimen is placed into a 15mm bag, and the surgical area is inspected for hemostasis.
